reports.title: Municipal Budget, FY 2020–21 through FY 2026–27
reports.summary: A side-by-side reading of seven years of Andover budgets — General Government, the Andover Elementary School board budget, and the RHAM regional levy — with comparison against CPI inflation and Social Security COLAs. Includes year-by-year detail through Town Meeting and the May 5 referendum.

reports.title: RHAM Class Size in Context
reports.summary: What RHAM's class sizes actually are today, measured directly from the master schedule, compared to peer Connecticut secondary regional districts both historically (CT Mirror's 2012–13 dataset) and today (NCES 2024–25). Examines the gap between RHAM's enrollment decline and its staffing decline as the source of currently small classes.

reports.title: RHAM Per-Pupil Spending in Context
reports.summary: Eleven years of Net Current Expenditures Per Pupil for RHAM and the seven other Connecticut regional districts that serve only secondary grades, with comparison against CPI-U inflation and the Social Security COLA. The headline +96.6% per-pupil increase over the decade is among the steepest in the peer group, but is overwhelmingly a denominator effect: enrollment fell 36.5% while total operating spending grew slower than inflation. Despite that, RHAM still ranks 6th of 8 on per-pupil spending in 2024–25.

reports.title: Andover's Per-Pupil Spending in Context
reports.summary: How Andover Elementary School's per-pupil spending compares to the 44 other Connecticut public school districts that share its structural profile: small-town, elementary-only districts (K–6 or K–8, enrollment under 2,000) that send middle- and high-school students to a regional district or by tuition contract. Built from the Connecticut State Department of Education's EdSight Per Pupil Expenditures report for school year 2024–25. Finds AES at the bottom of the rankings on essentially every measure of administrative spending.

reports.title: AES Preschool — Financial Analysis
reports.summary: Revenue, cost, and net-position figures for the Andover Elementary School preschool program: FY 2024–25 actuals from the audited financial filings, and FY 2025–26 anticipated figures from the Board of Education's monthly packets. Methodology and known data gaps are described inline.

reports.title: When Small Numbers Become Names
reports.summary: Why the disputed preschool staff-benefit figure cannot be released — and why that refusal is the law rather than a stonewall. Walks through how small-cell aggregation can re-identify individuals, which privacy law applies on the student side (FERPA) and the staff side (Connecticut FOIA), and what each does and does not permit.

reports.title: AES Preschool Structure: Why Four Classrooms?
reports.summary: Recreates and explains the classroom-by-classroom diagrams Superintendent Dr. Valerie Bruneau presented in her May 7, 2026 video, showing why the AES preschool cannot be run with two or three classrooms at its current enrollment — and connecting that structural picture to the program's finances.

reports.title: Steve Weir's Hidden Business: What the Public Record Shows
reports.summary: A public-records analysis of the active business holdings of State Rep. Steve Weir (R-55), built from Connecticut Secretary of State filings, the Glastonbury and Hebron assessor databases, the Connecticut Apartment Association's own member directory, and contemporaneous reporting from CT Mirror and NBC Connecticut. Identifies seven business entities tied to him personally — including the $1.69M industrial parcel at 60 Village Place in Glastonbury that he himself describes as where he spends most of his time outside the legislature — and traces the May 6, 2026 demise of SB 274, the Concierge Apartments response bill, which never received a House vote after he and other Republicans threatened to run the clock out. Includes a four-session pattern review of his housing votes and a careful assessment of the Connecticut ethics-law question.
detail: A public-records analysis of Rep. Weir's business holdings — built from Connecticut Secretary of State filings, Glastonbury and Hebron assessor records, OpenCorporates, and contemporaneous news reporting. Identifies seven CT business entities tied to him, including the $1.69M industrial parcel at 60 Village Place in Glastonbury that he himself describes as where he spends most of his time outside the legislature; documents his board seat on the Connecticut Apartment Association; and traces his consistent pattern of votes against contested tenant-protective housing legislation across four sessions.

The report's centerpiece is the May 6, 2026 demise of SB 274 — the Concierge Apartments response bill — which never received a recorded House vote after Republicans threatened to run the clock out on the final day of session. Includes a careful assessment of the Connecticut ethics-law question (CGS § 1-85 almost certainly permits the vote; disclosure is the load-bearing obligation, and Weir's official biography omits the commercial real estate entirely).

reports.title: $630,000 in Town Aid and One Vote Against It
reports.summary: A walkthrough of Rep. Steve Weir's (R-55) May 2, 2026 vote on SB 1, the FY 2026–27 budget adjustment that directs roughly $270 million in new K-12 and municipal aid to Connecticut towns. Using the Lamont administration's town-by-town breakdown, the report estimates approximately $630,000 of that aid is headed to the five towns of the 55th. The bill passed 127–21; Weir voted no. Documents that the vote was not party-line — 26 of 47 voting Republicans voted yes, including neighbors from the 8th District — and points readers to Weir's own statements for the reasoning.
detail: A walkthrough of Weir's May 2, 2026 vote on SB 1, the FY 2026–27 budget adjustment that directs roughly $270 million in new K-12 and municipal aid to Connecticut towns. Using the Lamont administration's town-by-town breakdown and 2024 turnout shares for the split-town portions of Bolton and Glastonbury, the report estimates that approximately $630,000 of that aid is headed to the five towns of the 55th. The bill passed 127–21. Weir voted no.

The report documents that the vote was not party-line: 26 of 47 voting Republicans voted yes, including neighbors from the 8th District. Weir's no was a member-level vote, not a leadership signal. The piece makes no claim about his reasoning — it documents the vote, the money, and the caucus split, and points readers to Weir's own statements for the why.

reports.title: Where Weir Sits in the House
reports.summary: Where Rep. Steve Weir (R-55) falls on the ideological spectrum of the Connecticut House, and how that compares to the partisan lean of the district he represents. Every member is placed on a single liberal-to-conservative scale built from their recorded roll-call votes using W-NOMINATE, a standard spatial method, for the 2025–2026 term and, for comparison, 2023–2024. Each district's partisan lean is a recency- and turnout-weighted average of its towns' two-party vote for President, U.S. Senate, U.S. House, and Governor across the 2012–2024 general elections. The findings are stable across both of Weir's terms: he votes among the most conservative members of the House, to the right of his own party's leader, while the 55th leans Democratic in statewide and federal elections.
detail: Places Rep. Weir on a single liberal-to-conservative scale built only from recorded House roll-call votes using W-NOMINATE — no position is assigned by hand; the scale emerges from who votes with whom — for the 2025–2026 term and, for comparison, 2023–2024. Each of the 151 districts' partisan lean is estimated independently from a dozen years of election returns (President, U.S. Senate, U.S. House, and Governor, 2012–2024), aggregated from towns to districts by 2020 census population and cross-checked against 2024 State House turnout.

Putting the two measurements side by side shows both where Weir sits among his colleagues and how that compares to where his district sits among all 151. The finding is stable across both of his terms: he votes among the most conservative members of the House, to the right of his own party's leader, while the 55th leans Democratic at the top of the ticket. All data inputs, outputs, and the R source are bundled with the report.

reports.title: Steve Weir's House Floor Votes, 2023–2026
reports.summary: Every recorded House floor vote Rep. Steve Weir (R-55) cast in the 2023, 2024, 2025, and 2026 regular sessions, compiled directly from the Connecticut General Assembly's roll-call PDFs and presented as a single sortable table organized by session. Each row links out to the bill's tracking page, the official roll-call PDF, and (where available) the bill text, and shows both the chamber-wide totals and the party-caucus splits — with rows outlined where Weir voted against the majority of his own caucus. CSV and JSON downloads of the full dataset are available alongside the HTML.
detail: Every recorded House floor vote Rep. Weir cast in the 2023, 2024, 2025, and 2026 regular sessions, compiled directly from the Connecticut General Assembly's roll-call PDFs and presented as a single sortable table organized by session. Each row links out to the bill's tracking page, the official roll-call PDF, and (where available) the bill text, and shows both the chamber-wide totals and the party-caucus splits — with rows outlined where Weir voted against the majority of his own caucus. CSV and JSON downloads of the full dataset are available alongside the HTML.
